wlp_eligibility_recipients: &wlp_eligibility_recipients
#  - venkateswarareddy.nakka@scdhhs.gov
#  - ramteja.motupalli@scdhhs.gov

email:
  sender: &sender dev_doh@scdhhs.gov
  recipients:
        - ArunKumar.Nimmoju@scdhhs.gov
    #    - NagaSravanthi.Nidumolu@scdhhs.gov
    #    - Venkateswarareddy.nakka@scdhhs.gov
    #    - chakradhar.kaitepalli@scdhhs.gov
  env: DEV

# Source.connection.type should be one of ORACLE or SQLSERVER

jobs:
  - job_name: INGEST_ONBASE_JOB
    job_type: INGEST
    description: "Loads all tables(Applications, CuramAssessmentQueues, Form1716, FormLTC, ReturnedMail, Reviews,TrailingDocuments) 
                  from OnBase Clemson DB to A4P_OnBase database."
    module_type: WLP
    source:
      type: database
      connection:
        type: SQLSERVER
        url: $CS-ONBASE-CLOUD$
        dbname: OnBase
        schema_prefix: hsi
        auth: WINDOWS_AUTH
      tables:
        - query: $BASE_DIR$/sql_ingest_queries/onbase/Applications.sql
          target_table_name: dbo.Applications
        - query: $BASE_DIR$/sql_ingest_queries/onbase/CuramAssessmentQueues.sql
          target_table_name: dbo.CuramAssessmentQueues
        - query: $BASE_DIR$/sql_ingest_queries/onbase/Form1716.sql
          target_table_name: dbo.Form1716
        - query: $BASE_DIR$/sql_ingest_queries/onbase/FormLTC.sql
          target_table_name: dbo.FormLTC
        - query: $BASE_DIR$/sql_ingest_queries/onbase/ReturnedMail.sql
          target_table_name: dbo.ReturnedMail
        - query: $BASE_DIR$/sql_ingest_queries/onbase/Reviews.sql
          target_table_name: dbo.Reviews
        - query: $BASE_DIR$/sql_ingest_queries/onbase/TrailingDocuments.sql
          target_table_name: dbo.TrailingDocuments
        - query: $BASE_DIR$/sql_ingest_queries/onbase/users.sql
          target_table_name: dbo.Users
    destination:
      type: database
      connection:
        type: SQLSERVER
        url: $CS-SQL-SYSDOH$
        dbname: A4P_OnBase
        schema_prefix: dbo
        auth: WINDOWS_AUTH
    options:
      batch_size: 10000
      overwrite: true

  #WLP Ingest from OnBase

  - job_name: INGEST_WLP_ONBASE_JOB
    job_type: INGEST

                  from OnBase Clemson DB to A4P_OnBase database."

        type: SQLSERVER

        dbname: OnBase  #OnBase
        schema_prefix: hsi

        - query: $BASE_DIR$/sql_ingest_queries/onbase/WLPConfigTeams.sql

  # SOR Stored procedure execution
  - job_name: SOR_WLP_CREATE_CO
    job_type: SOR
    description: "Executes stored procedure dbo.spWMRequestBatchCreate for Case Ownership view creation in A4P_WLP."
    module_type: WLP
    source:
      type: SQL_EXEC_SP
      sp_names:
        - sp_name: dbo.spWMRequestBatchCreate
          dbname: A4P_WM
          execute_at: "6:00 AM"
          include_response_in_email: true
    destination:
      type: database
      connection:
        type: SQLSERVER
        url: $CS-SQL-SYSDOH$
        dbname: A4P_WLP
        schema_prefix: dbo
        auth: WINDOWS_AUTH
    options:
      batch_size: 100000
      send_sms: true


  - job_name: SOR_WLP_OUTBOUND
    job_type: SOR
    description: "Executes stored procedures(dbo.spBatchCreate, dbo.spBatchSend)  for WLP report generation and outbound file creation, including CSV export to SFTP."
    module_type: WLP
    source:
      type: SQL_EXEC_SP
      sp_names:
        - sp_name: dbo.spBatchCreate
          dbname: A4P_WLP
          execute_at: "6:00 AM"
        - sp_name: dbo.spBatchSend
          consider_input_from_previous_sp: true
          params: {
            "BatchId": "0"  # Index position of the previous stored procedure returned result set
          }
          dbname: A4P_WLP
          execute_at: "6:00 AM"
          generate_csv_report: true
          destination:
            type: sftp_write
            connection:
              url: $CS-SFTP-ONBASE$  # Azure Key Vault entry, format: sftp://user:pass@host:port
              local_file_path: "E:/DOH_Shared/sftp/uploads"
              remote_dir: "/UPLOAD"
            options:
              format: csv
              delimiter: "|"
              header: true
              filename_format: "ROW[0].csv"
              batch_size: 10000
              overwrite: true
    #            type: network_share
    #            connection:
    #              path: //$CS-FILE-SHARE-SYSDOH$/WLP/POC/
    #              domain: your_domain
    #              user: share_user
    #              password: share_password
    #            options:
    #              format: csv
    #              delimiter: "|"
    #              header: true
    #              filename_format: "ROW[0].csv"
    #              batch_size: 10000
    #              overwrite: true


    destination:
      type: database
      connection:
        type: SQLSERVER
        url: $CS-SQL-SYSDOH$
        dbname: A4P_WLP
        schema_prefix: dbo
        auth: WINDOWS_AUTH
    options:
      batch_size: 100000

  # ingest WLP Response Files SFTP:
  - job_name: INGEST_WLP_INBOUND_FROM_SFTP
    job_type: INGEST
    description: "Ingests WLP_DTO response files from SFTP to A4P_WLP SQL Server DB."
    module_type: WLP
    source:
      type: SFTPCSV
      connection:
        url: $CS-SFTP-ONBASE$  # Azure Key Vault entry, format: sftp://user:pass@host:port
      files:
        - filename_pattern: "WLP_DTO.*.txt.*"         # Wildcard for timestamped CSV files
          remote_path: "/DOWNLOAD"       # Remote SFTP folder path
          local_download_path: "E:/DOH_Shared/sftp/downloads"   # Local temp folder
          target_table_name: "WLPBatchesInbound"    # SQL Server table name
          mode: "append"                           # append or truncate
          delete_after_download: false              # optional deletion
          delimiter: "|"                              # NEW � specify delimiter for this file
          has_header: false
          expected_file_count: 5
    destination:
      type: database
      connection:
        url: $CS-SQL-SYSDOH$     # SQL Server connection string (from Azure Key Vault)
        dbname: A4P_WLP  # Target database name
    options:
      batch_size: 1000

  - job_name: WLP_TASK_1_EXTRACT_OBJECT_IDS  #Step 1 - Extract TaskObjectIds from WLPTasks table to a CSV file
    job_type: EXTRACT
    description: "Extracts distinct TaskObjectIds from WLPTasks table to a CSV file for further processing. Target: CSV file."
    source:
      type: database
      connection:
        type: SQLSERVER
        url: $CS-SQL-SYSDOH$
        dbname: A4P_WLP
        schema_prefix: dbo
        auth: WINDOWS_AUTH
      tables:
        - query: select distinct t.TaskObjectId from dbo.WLPTasks t left join dbo.WLPStatuses s on t.[Status] = s.[StatusName] where isnull(s.[StatusFinal], 0) = 0
    destination:
      type: network_share
      connection:
        path: "E:/DOH_Shared/WLPTasks/TaskObjectIds"
        is_local_download_path: true  # There is an upload to a network share with SMB protocol. Which is not needed for local paths.
    options:
      format: csv
      delimiter: ","
      header: true
      filename_format: TaskObjectId_Extract_%Y-%m-%d_%H%M_random().csv
      batch_size: 10000
      overwrite: true

  - job_name: WLP_TASK_2_INGEST_LATEST_STATUS #Step 2, got this OnBase Query from meeting recordings
    job_type: INGEST
    description: "Ingests latest status for TaskObjectIds from OnBase_Prod to WLPSystemTaskStatuses (target_table_name: dbo.WLPSystemTaskStatuses) in A4P_WLP."
    source:
      type: database
      connection:
        type: SQLSERVER
        url: $CS-SQL-SYSONBASE-CLEMSON$
        dbname: OnBase_Prod
        schema_prefix: hsi
        auth: BASIC_AUTH
      tables:
        - query: "select t.objectId TaskOBjectId, wl.attr1652 Worklane, t.attr1687 CaseId, t.attr1654 [Status] 
                    from hsi.rmobjectinstance1057 t
                    left join hsi.rmobjectinstance1053 wl on t.fk1688  = wl.objectid and wl.activestatus = 0 
                    where t.activestatus = 0
                    and t.objectid in (?)"
          consider_input_from_local_csv: true
          local_csv_path: "E:/DOH_Shared/WLPTasks/TaskObjectIds/TaskObjectId_Extract_*.csv"
          target_table_name: dbo.WLPSystemTaskStatuses
          batch_size: 50
    destination:

        type: SQLSERVER

        dbname: A4P_WLP
        schema_prefix: dbo
        auth: WINDOWS_AUTH
    options:
      batch_size: 50000

  # IPP Jobs - Step 1 - Ingesting the Source Data from SFTP
  - job_name: INGEST_LLR_SOURCE_CSV
    job_type: INGEST
    description: "Ingests LLR files from SFTP to A4P_LLR SQL Server DB."
    module_name: IPP
    source:
      type: SFTPCSV
      connection:
        url: $CS-SFTP-SCLLR-QA$  #DEV SFTP # Azure Key Vault entry, format: sftp://user:pass@host:port
      files:
        - filename_pattern: "CDHHSExport_*.csv"         # Wildcard for timestamped CSV files
          remote_path: "/prod"       # Remote SFTP folder path
          local_download_path: "E:/DOH_Shared/sftp/downloads"   # Local temp folder
          target_table_name: "LLR_PY"    # SQL Server table name
          mode: "append"                           # append or truncate
          delete_after_download: false              # optional deletion
          delimiter: ","                              # NEW � specify delimiter for this file
          has_header: true
          expected_file_count: 1
          sftp_retry_attempts: 3
          sftp_retry_interval_seconds: 30
    destination:
      type: database
      connection:
        url: $CS-SQL-SYSDOH$     # SQL Server connection string (from Azure Key Vault)
        dbname: A4P_LLR  # Target database name
    options:
      batch_size: 1000
      send_email_per_file: false
      email:
        recipients:
          - ArunKumar.Nimmoju@scdhhs.gov

  # Step 2 - Generating the IPP File to deliver to HHCD103
  - job_name: EXTRACT_IPP_LLR_PFF_FILE
    job_type: SOR
    description: "Executes stored procedures(dbo.spGetLLR_PY_TO_MMIS) for IPP PFF outbound file creation, including file export to SFTP."
    module_name: IPP
    source:
      type: SQL_EXEC_SP
      sp_names:
        - sp_name: dbo.spGetLLR_PY_TO_MMIS
          dbname: A4P_LLR
          generate_csv_report: true
          destination:
            type: sftp_write
            connection:
              url: $CS-SFTP-MMIS$
              remote_dir: "//HHCD103"
              local_file_path: "E:/DOH_Shared/sftp/uploads/mainframe"
              is_mainframe_pff_file_type: true
            options:
              record_format: fb
              logical_record_length: 191
              format: csv
              delimiter: ""
              header: false
              filename_format: "CDIR.NEWPRV"
              batch_size: 10000
              overwrite: true
              fail_on_zero_records: true
    destination:
      type: database
      connection:
        type: SQLSERVER
        url: $CS-SQL-SYSDOH$
        dbname: A4P_LLR
        schema_prefix: dbo
        auth: WINDOWS_AUTH
    options:
      batch_size: 10000
      email:
        recipients:
          - ArunKumar.Nimmoju@scdhhs.gov

  #Step 3: Update Timestamp for delivered records in Audit Table
  - job_name: UPDATE_LLR_TIMESTAMP
    job_type: SOR
    description: "Executes stored procedures to update time stamp "
    module_name: IPP
    source:
      type: SQL_EXEC_SP
      sp_names:
        - sp_name: dbo.spSetLLR_PY_TO_MMIS_Delivered
          dbname: A4P_LLR
          execute_at: "6:00 AM"
    destination:
      type: database
      connection:
        type: SQLSERVER
        url: $CS-SQL-SYSDOH$
        dbname: A4P_LLR
        schema_prefix: dbo
        auth: WINDOWS_AUTH
    options:
      batch_size: 10000

    module_type: DW_MMIS

      files:

  - job_name: INGEST_DW_MMIS_RJHEALTH
    job_type: INGEST
    description: "Ingests RJHEALTH XWALK History file from SFTP to DW_MMIS SQL Server DB."
    source:
      type: SFTPCSV
      connection:
        url: $CS-SFTP-RJHEALTH$  # Azure Key Vault entry, format: sftp://user:pass@host:port
      files:
        - filename_pattern: "*ReimbursementCode Xwalk History-CSV Format.zip"      # Wildcard for timestamped CSV files
          remote_path: "/XwalkHistory"       # Remote SFTP folder path
          local_download_path: "E:/DOH_Shared/sftp/downloads"   # Local temp folder
          target_table_name: "RJHEALTH"    # SQL Server table name
          mode: "append"                       # append or truncate
          use_private_key: "false"
          delete_after_download: false              # optional deletion
          delimiter: ","
          has_header: true
          expected_file_count: 1
          sftp_retry_attempts: 3
          sftp_retry_interval_seconds: 30
          unzip: true
          cleanup_zip_after_unzip: false
          latest_only: true                  # default false
          latest_strategy: name      # remote_mtime | name | local_mtime
          # optional: keep the top N newest (default 1)
          latest_keep_n: 1
          # where to look for date when inner file has none
          file_date_source: auto           # auto | inner | zip_name | parent_folder
    destination:
      type: database
      connection:
        url: $CS-SQL-SYSDOH$     # SQL Server connection string (from Azure Key Vault)
        dbname: DW_MMIS # Target database name\
    options:
      batch_size: 10000
      send_email_per_file: false

# Job to get latest records in reporting tables for Power BI reports related to Provider Checks and Provider receiving checks under BOPSS.
  - job_name: INGEST_BOPSS_PROVIDER_CHECKS_AND_PROVIDER_RECEIVING_CHECKS
    job_type: SOR
    description: "Executes stored procedure dbo.spGetProviderChecksReport_forPowerBI and dbo.spGetReportProviderReceivingChecksforPowerBI to update tables (target_table_name: dbo.Report_Provider_Receiving_Checks, dbo.Report_ProviderChecks_ContinuingChecks, dbo.Report_ProviderChecks_ProviderCheckStatus, dbo.Report_ProviderChecks_ProviderPayStatusNotE)
     with the latest records."
    source:
      type: SQL_EXEC_SP
      sp_names:
        - sp_name: dbo.spGetProviderChecksReport_forPowerBI
          dbname: DW_MMIS
          execute_at: "2:40 PM"
        - sp_name: dbo.spGetReportProviderReceivingChecksforPowerBI
          dbname: DW_MMIS
          execute_at: "2:40 PM"
    destination:
      type: database
      connection:
        type: SQLSERVER
        url: $CS-SQL-SYSDOH$
        dbname: DW_MMIS
        schema_prefix: dbo
        auth: WINDOWS_AUTH
    options:
      batch_size: 100000

  - job_name: INGEST_CLAIMS
    job_type: INGEST
    description: "Ingests DSU0059.CLMS claims file from SFTP, routes by doc_type at position 17, loads into 6 tables using respective COBOL copybooks"
    module_type: CLAIMS
    source:
      type: SFTP
      connection:
        url: $CS-SFTP-MMIS$
      files:
        - remote_path: "//HHCD103"
          filename_pattern: "DSU0059.CLMS"
          buffer_size: "524288"
          local_download_path: "E:/DOH_Shared/sftp/downloads/claims"
          delete_after_process: false
          is_bcp_load_needed: true
          mode: "truncate"
          get_matching_file: false
          combined_file_routing:
            lrecl: 2265
            doc_type:
              position: 17
            tail_ind:
              position: 39
              true_values: ["1"]
            routes:
              # For CLMS - doc_type only routing, order still matters for clarity
                - doc_type: "U"
                  cobol_copybook: "CLAIMS/ADO_ADJUSTMENT_CLAIM.xml"
                  target_table_name: "dbo.ADO_ADJUSTMENT_CLAIM"

                - doc_type: "Y"
                  cobol_copybook: "CLAIMS/UB04_BASIC_CLAIM.xml"
                  target_table_name: "dbo.UB04_BASIC_CLAIM"
                - doc_type: "G"
                  cobol_copybook: "CLAIMS/NHO_NURSE_HOME_CLAIM.xml"

                - doc_type: "D"
                  cobol_copybook: "CLAIMS/PHO_PHARMACY_CLAIM.xml"
                  target_table_name: "dbo.PHO_PHARMACY_CLAIM"
    destination:
      type: database
      connection:

        url: $CS-SQL-SYSDOH$
        dbname: DW_MMIS

        auth: WINDOWS_AUTH

Below is a **latest YAML example for regular (single-table) BCP flow** with **byte-read enabled**.

```yaml name=regular_bcp_byte_read_example.yaml
jobs:
  - job_name: INGEST_DW_MEMBER_UB04_BASIC_CLAIM
    job_type: INGEST
    description: "Regular BCP ingest (single copybook) using fixed-length byte-read (lrecl) to avoid 0x0A splits in COMP fields."
    source:
      type: SFTP
      connection:
        url: $CS-SFTP-MMIS$
      files:
        - remote_path: "//HHCD103"
          file_extension: ""
          buffer_size: "524288"
          mode: "truncate"                 # truncate | append | upsert
          local_download_path: "E:/DOH_Shared/sftp/downloads/claims"
          filename_pattern: "DSU0059.CLMS" # example file
          cobol_copybook: "CLAIMS/UB04_BASIC_CLAIM.xml"
          target_table_name: "dbo.UB04_BASIC_CLAIM"

          is_bcp_load_needed: true

          # --- NEW: regular BCP byte-read mode ---
          is_byte_read: true
          lrecl: 2265                       # MUST be the fixed record length for this file

          # Optional (only if needed)
          alternative_delimiter: false
          quote_char: "§"
          delimiter_value: "\x1F"

          delete_after_process: false

          # Only for mode: upsert
          # primary_key:
          #   - <PK_COL1>
          #   - <PK_COL2>

    destination:
      type: database
      connection:
        type: SQLSERVER
        url: $CS-SQL-SYSDOH$
        dbname: DW_MMIS
        schema_prefix: dbo
        auth: WINDOWS_AUTH
    options:
      batch_size: 100000
      overwrite: true
```

Notes to confirm before you commit:
- Ensure `lrecl` matches the actual file’s logical record length (bytes).
- Don’t set `is_byte_read: true` unless the file is truly fixed-length and you know the correct `lrecl`.
- Combined ingestion remains separate and should continue using `combined_file_routing.lrecl` (not this `is_byte_read` path).

usually how much time it will take for 3.5 M records in global temp table to merge with main table 
as you already have the logic from sql_upsert_utils ?

There isn’t a single fixed time—it depends mostly on **(a)** how many of the 3.5M are *updates vs inserts*, **(b)** what indexes exist on the target table (especially on the PKs), and **(c)** SQL Server resources (CPU/IO/log throughput). But with the MERGE you have in `sql_upsert_utils.py`, these are realistic ranges.

## What your MERGE is doing (important for timing)
Your MERGE:

1. Checks duplicates in the temp table by PK (`GROUP BY ... HAVING COUNT(*) > 1`)
2. Builds `src_eligible` = temp rows excluding duplicate-key rows
3. Runs a single `MERGE target USING src_eligible ON pk-match`
4. For matched rows: updates **all non-key columns**
5. For not matched: inserts full row
6. Outputs `$action` for every affected row (extra overhead)

So the “cost drivers” are: large join on PK, logging for updates/inserts, and updating many columns.

## Typical time ranges for 3.5M rows
Assuming PK columns are indexed properly on the **target** table (clustered PK or a good nonclustered index):

- **Mostly inserts (target table small / empty):** ~5–25 minutes  
- **Mostly updates (target already has most keys):** ~20–90+ minutes  
- **50/50 mix:** ~15–60 minutes

If the target PK is **not indexed**, it can explode to **hours** (because SQL Server has to scan target repeatedly / huge hash join spills).

If there are **many nonclustered indexes** on the target table, updates get slower because each updated row must maintain those indexes too.

## The biggest “hidden” factor: transaction log + recovery model
A MERGE of millions of rows is heavily logged (especially updates). If DW_MMIS is in **FULL** recovery and log backups aren’t keeping up, you can see major slowdowns. Even in SIMPLE recovery, it’s still log-heavy.

## Another factor: your MERGE “OUTPUT $action”
This forces SQL Server to produce an output row per affected row (millions). That adds noticeable overhead (CPU + tempdb/memory). It’s not always huge, but at 3.5M it can be material.

## What to watch in your current run (to estimate remaining time)
If you can, run this in SSMS while the merge is running:

- Check whether the session is waiting on IO/log:
  - `WRITELOG` (log bottleneck)
  - `PAGEIOLATCH_*` (data file IO bottleneck)
  - `CXPACKET/CXCONSUMER` (parallelism; not always bad)

Also look at:
- tempdb usage (MERGE often spills)
- log growth events

## Quick “rule of thumb”
For a reasonably tuned SQL Server with proper PK index on target:
- **3.5M MERGE** is commonly **~20–60 minutes**.

If after ~60–90 minutes it’s still running, that usually indicates:
- missing/inefficient PK index on target, or
- IO/log bottleneck, or
- the update touches many columns + many indexes, or
- parameter sniffing / bad plan (less likely here since it’s dynamic SQL).

If you paste the final merge summary log lines once it completes (inserted/updated counts + duplicate counts), I can tell you whether the runtime you saw matches expectations for your workload.
how to know what process is going under while the job is running

To see what SQL Server is doing **while your Python job is running**, use SSMS and watch the session that is executing the `MERGE` (and/or the `BCP` inserts).

Below are the most practical ways.

---

## 1) Find the running session + current SQL text

Run this in the **DW_MMIS** SQL Server (SSMS → New Query):

```sql name=watch_running_requests.sql
SELECT
    r.session_id,
    s.login_name,
    s.host_name,
    s.program_name,
    r.status,
    r.command,
    r.start_time,
    DATEDIFF(MINUTE, r.start_time, GETDATE()) AS running_minutes,
    r.cpu_time,
    r.total_elapsed_time,
    r.reads,
    r.writes,
    r.logical_reads,
    r.wait_type,
    r.wait_time,
    r.last_wait_type,
    r.blocking_session_id,
    SUBSTRING(t.text, (r.statement_start_offset/2)+1,
              (CASE WHEN r.statement_end_offset = -1
                    THEN LEN(t.text)
                    ELSE (r.statement_end_offset-r.statement_start_offset)/2 END) + 1) AS current_statement,
    t.text AS full_batch
FROM sys.dm_exec_requests r
JOIN sys.dm_exec_sessions s ON s.session_id = r.session_id
CROSS APPLY sys.dm_exec_sql_text(r.sql_handle) t
WHERE s.is_user_process = 1
ORDER BY r.total_elapsed_time DESC;
```

Look for a row where `current_statement` contains:
- `MERGE` or
- `SELECT TOP 0 * INTO tempdb..##...` or
- anything referencing your global temp table like `##RSRI_RSP_INFOMTN_temp_...`

Also `program_name` may show `bcp.exe` during the BCP phase.

---

```sql name=find_requests_by_temp_table_name.sql
SELECT

    s.host_name,
    s.program_name,
    r.status,
    r.command,
    r.start_time,
    DATEDIFF(MINUTE, r.start_time, GETDATE()) AS running_minutes,
    r.wait_type,
    r.blocking_session_id,
    t.text
FROM sys.dm_exec_requests r
JOIN sys.dm_exec_sessions s ON s.session_id = r.session_id
CROSS APPLY sys.dm_exec_sql_text(r.sql_handle) t
WHERE t.text LIKE '%##RSRI_RSP_INFOMTN_temp_20260305113916_87551%'
ORDER BY r.total_elapsed_time DESC;
```

That will usually show the exact MERGE statement (or the duplicate-check query) that’s running.

---

## 3) See blocking (if it’s “stuck” waiting)

```sql name=check_blocking.sql
SELECT
    r.session_id,
    r.blocking_session_id,
    r.wait_type,
    r.wait_time,
    r.wait_resource,
    t.text
FROM sys.dm_exec_requests r
CROSS APPLY sys.dm_exec_sql_text(r.sql_handle) t
WHERE r.blocking_session_id <> 0
ORDER BY r.wait_time DESC;
```

If `blocking_session_id` is not 0, your MERGE is blocked by another session.

---

## 4) Understand waits (what the server is waiting on)

If you see `wait_type`, here are common ones:

- `WRITELOG` → transaction log bottleneck (very common for MERGE/large updates)
- `PAGEIOLATCH_*` → slow disk reads
- `LCK_M_*` → blocking/locks
- `CXPACKET` / `CXCONSUMER` → parallelism (not always bad)
- `RESOURCE_SEMAPHORE` → memory pressure / query waiting for memory grant
- `TEMPDB`-related waits → spills to tempdb

---

## 5) Optional: Watch tempdb growth / log growth quickly

Transaction log usage (rough):

```sql name=log_space_usage.sql
DBCC SQLPERF(LOGSPACE);
```

Tempdb space (simple view):

```sql name=tempdb_space_usage.sql
SELECT
    SUM(user_object_reserved_page_count) * 8.0 / 1024 AS user_objects_mb,
    SUM(internal_object_reserved_page_count) * 8.0 / 1024 AS internal_objects_mb,
    SUM(version_store_reserved_page_count) * 8.0 / 1024 AS version_store_mb
FROM sys.dm_db_file_space_usage;
```

---

If you run **Query #1** and paste the row for the session doing the `MERGE` (especially `wait_type`, `running_minutes`, `reads/writes`, `current_statement`), I can tell you exactly what it’s doing and what it’s waiting on.

If you don’t have permissions for DMVs like `sys.dm_exec_requests`, you still have a few good ways to see what’s happening—just with less detail.

## 1) Ask DBA for one-time “observer” access (best option)
To monitor running queries you typically need **VIEW SERVER STATE** (server-level) or at least rights to see other sessions. Without it you’ll be blind during MERGE.

What to ask for:
- Grant `VIEW SERVER STATE` (temporary is fine), or
- DBA runs the monitoring queries for you and shares: wait type, blocking, % complete, tempdb/log usage.

This is the normal/clean solution.

---

## 2) What you can do with no DMV permissions (from your side)

### A) Watch from the application logs (what you already do)
In your case, once the log prints:
- `Using primary keys: ...`
the next log line you’ll see is only after the MERGE finishes (or errors).

So “no log output” here usually means SQL is still executing the MERGE.

### B) Check if the global temp table still exists (may work, depends on permissions)
Sometimes you can run:

```sql name=check_temp_table_exists.sql
SELECT OBJECT_ID('tempdb..##RSRI_RSP_INFOMTN_temp_20260305113916_87551') AS obj_id;
```

- If you get a value (not NULL), temp table still exists (MERGE may or may not still be running).
- If NULL, temp table is gone (MERGE finished and cleanup ran, or job crashed and session ended).

This doesn’t require DMV access, just basic SELECT ability; but in some environments even `OBJECT_ID(tempdb..##...)` might be restricted.

### C) Check target table row counts changing (may work)
If you can query the target table:

```sql name=check_target_count.sql
SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_rows FROM dbo.RSRI_RSP_INFOMTN;
```

If counts are increasing while the job runs, it suggests inserts are happening.  
But note: MERGE runs in a single transaction; depending on isolation/logging, you might not see intermediate changes until commit.

---

## 3) Add lightweight “progress logging” to your pipeline (works without DB permissions)
Since you control the code, the best long-term approach is to add timing logs around each major step in `merge_upsert_from_global_temp`, for example:

- start duplicate check
- start export duplicates
- start MERGE
- MERGE completed (insert/update counts)
- drop temp table

This won’t show SQL-level waits, but it will tell you exactly **which stage** is taking time.

If you want, I can show exactly where to add these logs in `sql_upsert_utils.merge_upsert_from_global_temp` (very small change, safe).

---

## 4) Practical expectation for your current run
You already successfully:
- generated 6 CSV chunks
- BCP’d ~3.83M rows into the global temp table
Now the MERGE is the heavy part and can easily take **tens of minutes to over an hour** depending on indexes/log throughput.

Everything **looks good to go ahead** for both flows (regular BCP and combined ingestion).

One correction / alignment needed before you commit:
- In **`copybook_parser.upload_pff_with_copybook_to_mssql()`** you read **`is_byte_read`** and **`lrecl`** from `source.files[*]`.  
  So the YAML must set `is_byte_read: true` and `lrecl: <number>` **inside the file entry**, not somewhere else.

Below is the **latest YAML example** for a **regular (single-table) BCP job** with **byte-read enabled**:

```yaml name=regular-bcp-byte-read-example.yaml
jobs:
  - job_name: INGEST_SOME_MAINFRAME_FILE
    job_type: INGEST
    description: "Regular BCP ingestion using byte-read (fixed LRECL) to avoid 0x0A issues in COMP fields."
    source:
      type: SFTP
      connection:
        url: $CS-SFTP-MMIS$
      files:
        - remote_path: "//HHCD103"
          file_extension: ""
          buffer_size: "524288"
          local_download_path: "E:/DOH_Shared/sftp/downloads/some_folder"
          filename_pattern: "SOME.FILE.NAME"
          cobol_copybook: "./SOMEFOLDER/SOME_COPYBOOK.xml"
          target_table_name: dbo.SOME_TARGET_TABLE
          delete_after_process: false

          # REQUIRED for BCP path
          is_bcp_load_needed: true

          # MODE (truncate/append/upsert)
          mode: "truncate"

          # NEW: byte-read mode (fixed-record read)
          is_byte_read: true
          lrecl: 2265

          # Optional overrides (only if needed)
          # alternative_delimiter: false
          # quote_char: "§"
          # delimiter_value: "\x1F"

          # Only for upsert
          # primary_key:
          #   - COL1
          #   - COL2

    destination:
      type: database
      connection:
        type: SQLSERVER
        url: $CS-SQL-SYSDOH$
        dbname: DW_MMIS
        schema_prefix: dbo
        auth: WINDOWS_AUTH
    options:
      batch_size: 100000
      overwrite: true
```

references.name: copybook_parser.py

messages.content:
The reason you **don’t see NULLs for `PROV_SERVICE_1`** is:

1) In `parse_source_line()` you set **string fields** like this:

```python
value = str(raw_value.strip())
if is_replace_empty_string_with_null and value == "":
    value = None
```

So `PROV_SERVICE_1` becomes `None` correctly **when it is all spaces**.

2) But in `_migrate_data_bcp_style_local_csv()` (BCP path) you build the CSV row like this:

```python
row = [record.get(col, "") for col in column_names]
```

So when `record[col]` is `None`, `record.get(col, "")` returns `None` (not `""`)—however the `csv.writer` will serialize `None` as an **empty field**.

3) **BCP does not automatically convert empty fields to NULL** unless you explicitly tell it how. With your command you use `-w` and `-t <delimiter>`, but you do **not** pass anything like “treat empty fields as NULL”. So SQL Server ends up receiving `''` (empty string) for `varchar` columns (or it may be interpreted as empty string by the bulk load), not `NULL`.

That’s why you’re still seeing blanks/empty string for `PROV_SERVICE_1` even though parsing is trying to turn it into `None`.

---

## How to confirm this quickly (no code change needed)
Check the loaded data:

```sql name=check_null_vs_empty.sql
SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS total,
  SUM(CASE WHEN PROV_SERVICE_1 IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_count,
  SUM(CASE WHEN PROV_SERVICE_1 = '' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS empty_string_count,
  SUM(CASE WHEN LTRIM(RTRIM(PROV_SERVICE_1)) = '' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS blank_or_empty_count
FROM dbo.MMIS_PROVIDER;
```

If `empty_string_count` is high and `null_count` is 0 → exactly this BCP behavior.

---

## Also: you have a separate YAML mismatch
In the job you pasted, you have:

- Copybook defines record `storage-length="1377"`
- Your job doesn’t set `is_byte_read`/`lrecl`, so it’s reading as text lines.

If this file is truly fixed-length and might include binary/COMP bytes, you should set byte-read like:

```yaml name=mmis-provider-byte-read.yaml
is_bcp_load_needed: true
is_byte_read: true
lrecl: 1377
```

(Only if the physical file is fixed 1377 bytes per record.)

---

## Why only this column looks wrong?
It’s not only `PROV_SERVICE_1`. Any `varchar` field that is “empty” after `.strip()` will become an empty CSV field, and BCP will typically land it as `''`, not `NULL`.

So the pipeline is doing “replace empty with None” in Python, but the **BCP stage loses that distinction** unless you implement a NULL representation strategy for BCP.

---

When you share your job run output (counts + a small sample showing the raw value for `PROV_SERVICE_1`), I can pinpoint whether it’s:
- parsing not producing `None`, or
- CSV writing producing empty field, or
- BCP inserting as empty string.

'ZZ6550',
'ZZ7015',
'ZZ7020',
'ZZ7031',
'ZZ7104')

Your SQL results actually prove what’s happening:

- `null_count = 156,253`
- `empty_string_count = 0`
- `blank_or_empty_count = 0`

So **SQL Server is storing real NULLs** (good), and it is **not storing empty strings**.

But when you run:

```sql
select PROV_NUMBER, PROV_SERVICE_3 ...
```

SSMS shows NULLs as “blank cells”, so it *looks like spaces/empty*, even though they are NULL.

To confirm visually, run this:

```sql name=confirm_null_display.sql
SELECT
  PROV_NUMBER,
  CASE WHEN PROV_SERVICE_3 IS NULL THEN '<<NULL>>'
       WHEN PROV_SERVICE_3 = '' THEN '<<EMPTY>>'
       WHEN LTRIM(RTRIM(PROV_SERVICE_3)) = '' THEN '<<SPACES>>'
       ELSE '<<VALUE>>'
  END AS PROV_SERVICE_3_state,
  DATALENGTH(PROV_SERVICE_3) AS PROV_SERVICE_3_bytes,
  LEN(PROV_SERVICE_3) AS PROV_SERVICE_3_len,
  PROV_SERVICE_3
FROM dbo.d_MMIS_PROVIDER
WHERE PROV_NUMBER IN ('ZZ6550','ZZ7015','ZZ7020','ZZ7031','ZZ7104');
```

You should see `<<NULL>>` for those rows, and `DATALENGTH` will be `NULL` as well.

---

## Why Notepad++ shows `NULNULNUL...` (your screenshot)
That screenshot (shows `NUL` blocks) means the file contains **0x00 bytes** in that area.

That typically happens when:
- the file is not a pure text “printable” file,
- or it contains binary/computational fields,
- or it’s EBCDIC-to-ASCII converted imperfectly,
- or it’s fixed-length records padded with 0x00 (null bytes), not spaces.

### Important consequence for your pipeline
Your parsing uses:

- reading with `encoding="cp1252", errors="replace"` (text mode), OR
- binary fixed-length mode (`lrecl`) then `.decode("cp1252", errors="replace")`

In either case, **0x00 bytes decode into `\x00` characters**, and then:

```python
raw_value.strip()
```

does **not** remove `\x00` (it only strips whitespace), so the value becomes something like `"\x00\x00\x00..."` which is **not equal to ""**, so your “replace empty string with null” logic will *not* trigger from parsing alone.

However, your SQL results show NULLs are landing, which suggests one of these is true in your run:
- That field region is actually spaces in the decoded line (not `\x00`), or
- the bytes are getting replaced/removed somewhere (less likely), or
- you’re querying a table (`dbo.d_MMIS_PROVIDER`) where those specific columns are actually NULL due to mapping/column order issues.

---

## Next checks (to separate “SSMS display” vs “real spaces vs NUL bytes”)

### 1) Confirm whether it’s NULL or contains `\x00`
Run:

```sql name=detect_nul_bytes.sql
SELECT TOP 20
  PROV_NUMBER,
  PROV_SERVICE_3,
  DATALENGTH(PROV_SERVICE_3) AS bytes,
  CHARINDEX(CHAR(0), PROV_SERVICE_3) AS has_nul_pos
FROM dbo.d_MMIS_PROVIDER
WHERE PROV_SERVICE_3 IS NOT NULL
  AND CHARINDEX(CHAR(0), PROV_SERVICE_3) > 0;
```

- If this returns rows → you truly have NUL characters stored in SQL (bad for downstream).
- If it returns none → you don’t have NULs in SQL; the “blank” you see is NULL display.

### 2) Confirm the “spaces” case
Run:

```sql name=find_space_only_values.sql
SELECT TOP 20
  PROV_NUMBER,
  '[' + PROV_SERVICE_3 + ']' AS wrapped,
  LEN(PROV_SERVICE_3) AS len,
  DATALENGTH(PROV_SERVICE_3) AS bytes
FROM dbo.d_MMIS_PROVIDER
WHERE PROV_SERVICE_3 IS NOT NULL
  AND PROV_SERVICE_3 <> ''
  AND LTRIM(RTRIM(PROV_SERVICE_3)) = '';
```

If this returns rows, then you truly have “spaces” stored (not NULL). But your earlier aggregate (`blank_or_empty_count = 0`) suggests it will return none.

---

## One more thing: your `copybook_parser.py` has `print()` debugging
In what you pasted, you have module-level prints and a global `count` printing from `parse_source_line()`. That’s fine for testing locally, but if you’re committing to Azure DevOps, that will spam logs.

```python name=copybook_parser.py
print("1 is_replace_empty_string_with_null: ", is_replace_empty_string_with_null)
...
print("2 is_replace_empty_string_with_null: ", is_replace_empty_string_with_null)
...
print("is_replace_empty_string_with_null: ", is_replace_empty_string_with_null)
```

---

### Tell me these 2 outputs and I’ll pinpoint the exact cause
1) Output of `confirm_null_display.sql` for your 5 providers  
2) Whether `detect_nul_bytes.sql` returns any rows

That will confirm if you’re only seeing **SSMS blank-for-NULL**, or if **NUL bytes are sneaking into SQL**.
Describe this image

I can describe what’s shown across the three screenshots (top to bottom):

```text name=image-description.txt
Image 1 (top screenshot)
- This is SQL Server Management Studio (SSMS) with a query that classifies PROV_SERVICE_3 as:
  <<NULL>>, <<EMPTY>>, <<SPACES>>, or <<VALUE>>,
  and also selects DATALENGTH(PROV_SERVICE_3) and LEN(PROV_SERVICE_3).
- The query filters by PROV_NUMBER in ('ZZ6550','ZZ7015','ZZ7020','ZZ7031','ZZ7104').
- In the results grid:
  - PROV_SERVICE_3_state shows <<VALUE>> for all 5 providers.
  - PROV_SERVICE_3_bytes = 6 and PROV_SERVICE_3_len = 6 for all 5.
  - The displayed PROV_SERVICE_3 column looks blank in SSMS even though the state says <<VALUE>> and length/bytes are 6.

Image 2 (middle screenshot)
- SSMS query checks for NUL characters inside PROV_SERVICE_3:
  SELECT TOP 20 PROV_NUMBER, PROV_SERVICE_3, DATALENGTH(PROV_SERVICE_3) AS bytes,
  CHARINDEX(CHAR(0), PROV_SERVICE_3) AS has_nul_pos
  FROM dbo.d_MMIS_PROVIDER
  WHERE PROV_SERVICE_3 IS NOT NULL
    AND CHARINDEX(CHAR(0), PROV_SERVICE_3) > 0;
- The results grid returns many rows.
- For each row shown:
  - bytes = 6
  - has_nul_pos = 1
  - PROV_SERVICE_3 appears blank in the grid.
- This indicates PROV_SERVICE_3 contains a CHAR(0) (NUL) at position 1 (and likely more NULs),
  not NULL and not spaces.

Image 3 (bottom screenshot)
- SSMS query tries to find rows where PROV_SERVICE_3 is NOT NULL,
  not an empty string, and trims to empty (space-only values):
  WHERE PROV_SERVICE_3 IS NOT NULL
    AND PROV_SERVICE_3 <> ''
    AND LTRIM(RTRIM(PROV_SERVICE_3)) = '';
- The results grid is empty (no rows returned),
  meaning there are no “space-only” values meeting that condition (at least in the sampled TOP 20 query).
```

Net takeaway from the images: **`PROV_SERVICE_3` is not NULL and not spaces; it contains NUL bytes (`CHAR(0)`)**, and SSMS displays those characters as blank even though `LEN/DATALENGTH` are 6.

what should i do now 
they are handled and stored as NULLS in sql in legacy we want in same way handling

You’re in the “NUL bytes” case, not the “spaces” case.

Your screenshots prove this:

- `DATALENGTH(PROV_SERVICE_3) = 6` and `LEN(PROV_SERVICE_3) = 6` for those rows
- `CHARINDEX(CHAR(0), PROV_SERVICE_3) = 1`
- So the value is basically `\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00` (or begins with NULs). SSMS renders that as blank, but it’s **not NULL**.

In legacy, they likely convert “all NULs” (or “blank after removing NULs”) to **NULL**.

## What to do (to match legacy behavior)

### Fix at the correct place: `parse_source_line()` (string fields)
When you slice a raw_value for *string* fields, normalize it by replacing NULs with spaces (or removing them), then strip. If after that it becomes empty, set it to `None` when `replace_empty_string_with_null` is enabled.

Add a helper like this inside `parse_source_line()` for non-numeric, non-date fields:

```python name=copybook_parser.py
def _clean_text_value(raw_value: str) -> str:
    # Convert embedded NULs to spaces (mainframe/binary padding) then strip.
    # You can also use .replace("\x00", "") if you prefer dropping them.
    return (raw_value or "").replace("\x00", " ").strip()
```

Then change both places where you set string values:

**Occurs > 1 branch (string field):**
```python name=copybook_parser.py
value = _clean_text_value(raw_value)
if is_replace_empty_string_with_null and value == "":
    value = None
```

**Occurs == 1 branch (string field):**
```python name=copybook_parser.py
value = _clean_text_value(raw_value)
if is_replace_empty_string_with_null and value == "":
    value = None
```

This will convert the `NULNULNUL...` into `""` (after strip) and then into `None` → and BCP will load it as an empty field.  
But we still need to ensure it becomes NULL in SQL…

### Important: ensure BCP loads empty fields as NULL for varchar
Right now, your BCP pipeline is writing an empty field in CSV. Depending on bulk load behavior, SQL Server often treats it as empty string for varchar. However your earlier aggregate query showed a lot of NULLs, so you already have some NULL conversion happening somewhere—BUT the NUL case is bypassing it because the string isn’t empty.

After the `_clean_text_value()` change, those NUL-only fields will become empty and then `None`, which will usually serialize as empty in CSV. To make it consistently land as NULL, the most reliable approach is to use a **BCP format file** or `BULK INSERT` with `KEEPNULLS`/`NULLIF`, but since you’re using the `bcp` CLI, the practical approach is:

- keep using your current pipeline, and confirm after the change that those columns become NULL (not `''` and not `CHAR(0)`).

Given your `empty_string_count = 0`, your environment likely already ends up storing them as NULL when the field is empty.

---

## Why this is happening only now
Because NUL bytes survive `.strip()`; they’re not whitespace. So your current “empty string → NULL” logic never triggers for NUL-padded fields.

Legacy likely had logic equivalent to:  
“treat CHAR(0) as blank/padding” → then apply empty-to-null.

---

## Quick verification queries after the change
After re-run ingestion, confirm:

```sql name=verify_no_nul.sql
SELECT TOP 20 *
FROM dbo.d_MMIS_PROVIDER
WHERE PROV_SERVICE_3 IS NOT NULL
  AND CHARINDEX(CHAR(0), PROV_SERVICE_3) > 0;
```

Should return **0 rows**.

And confirm NULL counts increased appropriately:

```sql name=verify_nulls.sql
SELECT
  SUM(CASE WHEN PROV_SERVICE_3 IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_count,
  SUM(CASE WHEN PROV_SERVICE_3 IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS not_null_count
FROM dbo.d_MMIS_PROVIDER;
```

---

## One more note (byte-read vs not)
Even if you do byte-read, you can still get NUL bytes. So this cleaning step is still needed to match legacy.

---

- it calls `upload_pff_with_copybook_to_mssql_direct_insert()`

So `_clean_text_value()` is applied to every non-numeric/non-date text field → includes:

## 2) BCP ingest (regular text line mode)
**Yes, it will happen.**

Flow:
- `upload_pff_with_copybook_to_mssql()` decides `is_bcp_load_needed = true`
- it chunks using `split_file_by_lines()`
- for each chunk, `_migrate_data_bcp_style_local_csv(...)` reads **text lines** and calls:

So the same `_clean_text_value()` logic runs, including the `\x00` replacement.

---

## 3) BCP ingest with byte stream mode (`is_byte_read=true`, `lrecl=...`)
**Yes, it will happen.**

Flow:
- `upload_pff_with_copybook_to_mssql()` decides `is_byte_read=true`
- it chunks using `split_file_by_fixed_lrecl(...)`
- then `_migrate_data_bcp_style_local_csv(..., lrecl=<value>)` reads the chunk in **binary fixed-length blocks**
- it **decodes** the raw bytes into a Python string (using cp1252 with errors=replace)
- then calls:
  - `record = parse_source_line(decoded_record, schema, ...)`

Because the decoded record can still contain embedded `\x00` characters (very common with fixed-width/binary-ish data), your `_clean_text_value()` will replace them before `.strip()` and before your “empty string to NULL” conversion.

### Important note (why you might still see blanks instead of NULL in SQL Server)
Even if `parse_source_line()` returns `None`, your BCP CSV writer currently does this:


So if `record[col]` is `None`, it becomes **empty string** in the CSV, not a NULL marker.  
BCP will then load that as `''` (empty string) for varchar columns, not SQL NULL.

So:
- `replace("\x00"," ")` **does happen**
- but “empty -> NULL” may still **not survive the CSV/BCP layer** unless you output a true NULL representation for BCP (different handling needed)

But to your direct question: **the `replace("\x00", " ")` executes in all 3 flows** because all 3 call `parse_source_line()`.

Yes — you can do it inline (directly where you currently do `raw_value.strip()`), you don’t *need* a separate helper method.

### What to change (minimal + consistent)
Wherever you currently parse text fields like this:

```python name=copybook_parser.py
value = str(raw_value.strip())
if is_replace_empty_string_with_null and value == "":
    value = None
```

Change it to this (inline replace + strip):

```python name=copybook_parser.py
value = str((raw_value or "").replace("\x00", " ").strip())
if is_replace_empty_string_with_null and value == "":
    value = None
```

Do this in **both places** in `parse_source_line()`:
1) the `occurs > 1` text-field block  
2) the `else` (single-occurrence) text-field block

That’s it.

### Why this fixes your `PROV_SERVICE_1` case
When you run **byte-read (`lrecl`)**, the line is read in binary and decoded with `errors="replace"`. If the original field is “blank”, it may contain **NUL bytes (`0x00`)** (common with fixed-length / binary-ish layouts), not spaces.  

- `strip()` does **not** remove `\x00`, so the value is not `""`
- replacing `\x00` with `" "` and then stripping makes it become `""`
- then your `replace_empty_string_with_null` logic correctly turns it into `None`
- and when writing CSV for BCP, Python `csv.writer` writes `None` as an empty field, which SQL Server imports as `NULL` (for character columns) in most BCP scenarios

### One more quick check (important)
Make sure your job/module actually has this set:

```yaml name=config-dev.yaml
options:
  replace_empty_string_with_null: true
```

In your earlier YAML snippet you showed it under `options`, which is correct for how your current `upload_pff_with_copybook_to_mssql()` reads it.

Yes—you can do it **inline** (no separate helper) and still keep the behavior consistent everywhere.

## 1) Using `.replace()` directly (no separate method)

In your old code you were doing:

```python name=copybook_parser.py
value = str(raw_value.strip())
if is_replace_empty_string_with_null and value == "":
    value = None
```

That **does not remove `\x00` (NUL)** bytes because `strip()` only removes whitespace (spaces, tabs, newlines), not NUL. So the value is not `""`, it’s something like `"\x00\x00\x00"` (or `"\x00 ..."`), so it never becomes `None`.

To fix it inline, replace NULs first, then strip:

```python name=copybook_parser.py
value = str(raw_value).replace("\x00", " ").strip()
if is_replace_empty_string_with_null and value == "":
    value = None
```

Do that in **both places** where text fields are parsed:
- occurs > 1 branch (`if occurs > 1: ... else:` inside)
- occurs == 1 branch

That’s it—no separate method needed.

---

## 2) Why the attached “NUL NUL NUL …” characters were not stripped in the old code

Your image shows literal **NUL characters** (byte `0x00`). They are **not whitespace**, so:

- `strip()` **does not remove them**
- `raw_value.strip()` still contains those NUL bytes
- so `value == ""` is false
- so your “replace empty string with null” logic never triggers

In other words, to your old code the field is **not empty**, it’s “a string containing NULs”.

That’s why you see the weird `NULNULNUL...` display and why it doesn’t turn into SQL `NULL`.

---

## 3) One more important point (BCP behavior)

Even if Python sets `value = None`, your BCP CSV writer currently does:

```python name=cobol_to_bcp_convertion.py
row = [record.get(col, "") for col in column_names]
```

So if `record[col]` is `None`, `record.get(col, "")` returns `None` (good), but the **csv module will write an empty field**.

**BCP treats empty fields as empty string by default**, not NULL, *unless you explicitly configure NULL handling* (format file / `-k` keep nulls / etc., depending on your approach and schema). So:

- Your Python-side `None` fix is necessary to remove the NULs and normalize empties,
- But whether the SQL column ends up as `NULL` vs `''` depends on your BCP options / table defaults / format.

If you want, tell me how you run `bcp` (exact command options) and whether the destination columns are `varchar` or `char`, and I can explain exactly what SQL Server will store for empty CSV fields in your current setup.

Yes — you *can* avoid a separate `_clean_text_value()` helper and just do a direct `.replace(...).strip()` inline wherever string fields are handled. Functionally it’s the same. The important part is: in **byte-read mode** you can get `\x00` (NUL) padding inside the decoded text; plain `.strip()` does **not** remove `\x00`, so you must convert it first (or treat it as empty) before checking `== ""`.

A minimal inline pattern you can use everywhere you currently do `raw_value.strip()`:

```python name=inline_cleaning_example.py
value = (raw_value or "").replace("\x00", " ").strip()
if is_replace_empty_string_with_null and value == "":
    value = None
```

---

## Why `PROV_SERVICE_1` didn’t become NULL earlier (regular BCP flow)
This is the usual root cause:

- COBOL fixed-width “blank” fields are often **spaces** → `.strip()` turns them into `""` → you convert to `None` ✅
- But in **byte-read/binary-ish scenarios**, some “blank” fields may decode to **`\x00` bytes** (NUL padding) → `.strip()` does **not** remove NUL → value is not `""` → you **don’t** convert to `None` ❌

So the `replace("\x00", " ")` (or `replace("\x00", "")`) is what makes the NULL conversion work reliably.

---

## Your EFT issue: why `EFT_BANK_ID_NO` and `EFT_TELEPHONE` become NULL while others load
Given your copybook + table, and that “all other columns load properly”, this pattern strongly indicates **position/length misalignment** (most common) or **byte-read decoding artifacts** (second most common), specifically affecting those two fields.

### 1) Copybook positions look correct *if* the file is truly 200 bytes fixed layout
From the XML:

- `EFT_NPI_KEY` position 1 length 10 → bytes 1–10
- `EFT_BANK_ID_NO` position 11 length 9 → bytes 11–19
- `EFT_ACCOUNT_NUMBER` position 20 length 17 → bytes 20–36
- `EFT_TELEPHONE` position 63 length 10 → bytes 63–72

Those are consistent.

So if those two are NULL but others aren’t, the most likely explanations are:

### 2) The actual file is not matching the XML for those two fields
Common reasons:
- The source file has **a record prefix**, RDW, ASA carriage control, or an extra flag byte(s) at the front.
- The file is EBCDIC converted inconsistently (some bytes become `\x00` or non-printables).
- The record has **different LRECL** than `storage-length="200"` (or the file includes separators you didn’t account for).

If there is *any* offset at the front (even 1–4 bytes), fields early in the record (like `EFT_BANK_ID_NO`) will be wrong first.

### 3) Your “replace empty with null” logic may be turning “bad decode” into empty
If those fields decode to `\x00\x00...` or other non-visible bytes, your cleaning step (especially if you replace NUL with spaces then strip) will produce `""` → becomes `None` → ends up NULL. That’s consistent with “data present but I see NULLs”.

So: the pipeline is likely **not extracting the right slice** for those fields.

---

## What to check (quick, high-confidence checks)
### A) Confirm actual record length (critical)
Take a single record in binary and confirm length is really 200 bytes (excluding CRLF):

- If you run with `is_byte_read=true`, you must set `lrecl: 200` (for EFT file) in YAML.
- If you do **not** set byte-read mode and the file has embedded bytes, line splitting could corrupt records.

### B) Verify there is no leading record header
If there’s an RDW-like 4-byte header, your XML positions are off by 4. Then:
- `EFT_BANK_ID_NO` would be reading the wrong bytes (often spaces/NUL) → NULL.
- Some later fields *might still look “okay-ish”* depending on content.

### C) Temporarily log raw slices for the two fields for 1–2 records
Not suggestions about redesign—this is the fastest way to prove misalignment:
- log `repr(raw_value)` for positions 11–19 and 63–72 before cleaning.

If you see mostly `\x00` or spaces, you’re slicing the wrong region.

---

## YAML for **regular BCP** with **byte read = true**
For a standard single-copybook ingestion (not combined routing), you want **all of these together** in the job’s `files:` entry:

- `is_bcp_load_needed: true`
- `is_byte_read: true`
- `lrecl: <record_length>` (must match copybook storage-length / actual LRECL)

Example for EFT (200-byte records):

```yaml name=example_ingest_eft_bcp_byte_read.yaml
- job_name: INGEST_DW_PROVIDER_EFT_PROV
  job_type: INGEST
  description: "Ingests EFT_PROV file from SFTP to DW_MMIS SQL Server DB using COBOL copybook."
  module_type: DW_PROVIDER
  source:
    type: SFTP
    connection:
      url: $CS-SFTP-MMIS$
    files:
      - remote_path: "//HHCD103"
        file_extension: ""
        buffer_size: "524288"
        local_download_path: "E:/DOH_Shared/sftp/downloads/provider"
        filename_pattern: "EFTPROV.REC"
        cobol_copybook: "EFT-PROV-RECORD.xml"
        target_table_name: dbo.EFT_PROV_RECORD
        delete_after_process: false

        # regular BCP flow flags:
        is_bcp_load_needed: true
        is_byte_read: true
        lrecl: 200
  destination:
    type: database
    connection:
      type: SQLSERVER
      url: $CS-SQL-SYSDOH$
      dbname: DW_MMIS
      schema_prefix: dbo
      auth: WINDOWS_AUTH
  options:
    batch_size: 20000
    overwrite: true
    replace_empty_string_with_null: true
```

Do the same for MMIS_PROVIDER with `lrecl: 1377` if the physical record length is 1377.

---

## “Does everything look good to go ahead and commit?”
Overall the flow is consistent **if**:
1) For every byte-read job, `lrecl` matches the physical LRECL of the file (not just what the XML says).
2) Your empty->NULL conversion handles `\x00` (either inline replace or helper).
3) You remove the debug `print()` statements you added earlier (those will flood logs in production and can slow runs).

The EFT NULLs you’re seeing are not a BCP problem—BCP is loading what’s in the CSV. The issue is almost certainly **record slicing alignment** (wrong LRECL / unexpected header / offset) causing those two fields to parse as “blank” after cleaning, which then becomes NULL.

messages.content:
### 1) “Replace empty strings with NULL” — why `PROV_SERVICE_1` is **not** becoming NULL (BCP path)

In your **BCP flow**, values are written to a **CSV** (UTF‑16) in `cobol_to_bcp_convertion._migrate_data_bcp_style_local_csv()`, and then loaded by **bcp**.

Key point: **BCP does not treat an empty field as NULL by default**.

- Your parser can set `value = None` for empty strings.
- But when you build the CSV row you do:

```python
row = [record.get(col, "") for col in column_names]
```

So even if the parser produced `None`, this converts it to `""` (empty field) in the CSV.

Then BCP loads that empty field into SQL Server as **empty string**, not NULL (for `varchar` columns), unless you explicitly tell BCP to treat blanks as NULL (or you emit a NULL marker BCP recognizes).

That’s why you’re seeing `PROV_SERVICE_1` as spaces/empty instead of NULL.

#### Also, why it can look like “spaces” instead of empty
In fixed-width mainframe files the “empty” value is usually **spaces** (and sometimes **NUL bytes** if you read binary / lrecl). Your `_clean_text_value()` approach is correct (strip + replace `\x00`), but after that you still end up writing `""` to CSV, and BCP loads it as empty string.

So the behavior is expected with the current BCP pipeline.

---

### 2) “Instead of a separate method can’t we just use replace directly?”
Yes. You don’t need a separate helper if you don’t want one. You can inline it where you currently do:

```python
value = str(raw_value.strip())
```

and change it to something like:

```python
value = str(raw_value.replace("\x00", " ").strip())
if is_replace_empty_string_with_null and value == "":
    value = None
```

That part is fine.

But **even with that**, you still won’t get SQL NULLs via BCP unless you also fix the **CSV writing / BCP options** (because `None -> ""` happens when writing the CSV row).

---

### 3) Why EFT file “group fields split and load properly”, but MMIS Provider “combined” isn’t (what’s actually different)

What you’re calling “split properly” in EFT is about **schema mapping / field extraction**, not NULL behavior.

- In `EFT-PROV-RECORD.xml`, the group items like `EFT_BANK_ID_NO` have children `EFT_BANK_ROUTING_NO` + `EFT_BANK_CHECK_DIGIT`.  
  Your `parse_cobol_xml()` only appends **leaf items** (items with no children), so it naturally produces the two leaf fields, which match your SQL columns.

- In `MMIS-PROVIDER.xml`, the “combined column” situation you mentioned earlier (like `PROV_SERVICE_1` or other “filler/combined structures”) is not the same issue as EFT grouping.
  The Provider copybook has many leaf fields too, so parsing is likely fine. The thing that’s *not* behaving is **NULL conversion**, not “splitting”.

If you mean a *different* “combined column” case (like `redefines` / multiple layouts at the same position), then the difference is:
- EFT does **not** use `redefines` in the same problematic way.
- Provider copybook includes `redefines` (`PROV_DEBIT_INFO` vs `PROV_DEBIT_SPACE`). In those cases, **both sets of leaf fields share the same bytes**. Your parser will populate both groups from the same raw positions, which is expected. SQL will get values for both unless your downstream logic chooses one interpretation.

But again: that’s separate from empty->NULL.

---

### 4) The real fix for NULLs in **BCP flow** (so you can confidently commit)

To truly get NULLs into SQL Server via BCP, you need one of these patterns:

#### Option A (most common): Use BCP “keep nulls / blanks as nulls” behavior
BCP has switches like:
- `-k` (“keep null values” – affects how bcp handles empty fields depending on format)  
- and/or using a **format file** to define NULL handling.

Your current `_insert_local_csv_to_sql_using_bcp_command()` does not use a format file and doesn’t pass `-k`. So empty fields become empty strings.

#### Option B: Emit a NULL marker BCP recognizes
This usually requires a **format file** that defines a `NULL` representation, or using `-c`/`-w` with a format file. Without a format file, BCP can’t reliably interpret a custom token like `\N` as NULL automatically.

#### Option C: Post-load update to convert empty strings to NULL
After BCP, run:

```sql
UPDATE dbo.MMIS_PROVIDER
SET PROV_SERVICE_1 = NULL
WHERE LTRIM(RTRIM(PROV_SERVICE_1)) = '';
```

This works, but it’s after-the-fact.

---

### 5) “Latest YAML for regular BCP with byte read true”
For a **regular (single-table) BCP ingestion** using your new `is_byte_read` + `lrecl`, the file-level config must include:

- `is_bcp_load_needed: true`
- `is_byte_read: true`
- `lrecl: <record-length>`
- (optional) `quote_char`, `delimiter_value`

Example job snippet (only the relevant part):

```yaml name=job-snippet.yaml
- job_name: INGEST_DW_PROVIDER_MMIS_PROVIDER_Updated
  job_type: INGEST
  description: "Ingests MMIS provider file from SFTP to DW_MMIS SQL Server DB using COBOL copybook."
  module_type: WLP
  source:
    type: SFTP
    connection:
      url: $CS-SFTP-MMIS$
    files:
      - remote_path: "//HHCD103"
        file_extension: ""
        buffer_size: "524288"
        local_download_path: "E:/DOH_Shared/sftp/downloads/provider"
        filename_pattern: "MMIS.PROVIDER.FILE"
        cobol_copybook: "DW_MMIS/MMIS-PROVIDER.xml"
        target_table_name: dbo.MMIS_PROVIDER
        delete_after_process: false
        is_bcp_load_needed: true
        is_byte_read: true
        lrecl: 1377
  destination:
    type: database
    connection:
      type: SQLSERVER
      url: $CS-SQL-SYSDOH$
      dbname: DW_MMIS
      schema_prefix: dbo
      auth: WINDOWS_AUTH
  options:
    batch_size: 100000
    overwrite: true
    replace_empty_string_with_null: true
```

Notes:
- `lrecl` should match the copybook top storage length for a fixed-record file (`MMIS_PROVIDER` shows `storage-length="1377"`). If the physical file includes record separators, your fixed-lrecl splitter already accounts for CR/LF.
- `target_table_name` should be the real table; in your pasted job you had `dbo.d_MMIS_PROVIDER` while the DDL you pasted is `dbo.MMIS_PROVIDER`. Make sure those match.

---

### 6) Quick “go / no-go” check based on what you shared

**Byte-read flow:** looks consistent (you added `split_file_by_fixed_lrecl()` and pass `lrecl` down into `_migrate_data_bcp_style_local_csv()`).

**However:** if your goal is “empty strings become NULL in SQL Server” for BCP loads, you are **not done yet** (because of the CSV row `record.get(col, "")` + BCP default behavior). You can commit byte-read safely, but you should not expect NULL conversion to work for BCP loads yet.

---

If you tell me which approach you want for NULLs (BCP switch/format file vs post-load update), I can give you the exact minimal code change consistent with your current pipeline (but you said “latest yaml” + check readiness, so I kept it focused on that and the root cause).

messages.content:
For **EFT_PROV_RECORD**, your copybook + your SQL table **do match the “split/group” expectation**, so the loader can populate the split columns correctly. For the **MMIS_PROVIDER** case (the earlier file), the part you’re calling “combined column” is behaving differently because of how the XML describes it (and how your parser expands `OCCURS`).

### Why EFT loads correctly (BANK ID NO + TELEPHONE)
In `EFT-PROV-RECORD.xml` you have **groups**:

- `EFT_BANK_ID_NO` (level-04 group) contains two children:
  - `EFT_BANK_ROUTING_NO` (numeric)
  - `EFT_BANK_CHECK_DIGIT` (numeric)

- `EFT_TELEPHONE` (level-04 group) contains three children:
  - `EFT_AREA_CODE`
  - `EFT_EXCHANGE`
  - `EFT_PHONE_NUMBER`

Your `parse_cobol_xml()` walks the XML recursively and **only appends leaf nodes** (items with no children). So it produces schema entries for:
- `EFT_BANK_ROUTING_NO`, `EFT_BANK_CHECK_DIGIT`
- `EFT_AREA_CODE`, `EFT_EXCHANGE`, `EFT_PHONE_NUMBER`
…which match your table columns exactly, so BCP loads them fine.

### Why the earlier “combined” field (MMIS_PROVIDER) didn’t split/load the way you expected
In `MMIS-PROVIDER.xml`, the “combined column” problem comes from **how that portion is modeled**:

You have this pattern:

- `PROV_SERVICE_1` (leaf)
- `PROV_RATE_SETS_1 occurs="12"` (group with leaf children `PROV_RATE_1` and `PROV_RATE_DT_1`)
- then repeated for `_2`, `_3`

Your parser expands OCCURS by suffixing **based on the leaf name**, not based on the parent group. So for `PROV_RATE_1` with occurs=12, the schema will generate:
- `PROV_RATE_1_01 ... PROV_RATE_1_12`
and similarly for dates.

That part is fine **only if** your SQL columns are named *exactly the same way*.

Where it breaks (for the earlier file) is typically one of these (and EFT doesn’t hit these edge cases):

1) **Column name mismatch between what the parser generates vs what SQL table has**
   - Example: parser generates `PROV_RATE_1_01`, but table column is `PROV_RATE_1_1` (or vice versa), or padded vs not padded.
   - EFT doesn’t have this complexity because its OCCURS items are simple and your table uses `_1.._6` without zero padding.

2) **Redefines/overlaps in the XML**
   - EFT does not use `redefines` in the parts you’re validating.
   - MMIS_PROVIDER has some `redefined/redefines` blocks (`PROV_DEBIT_INFO` / `PROV_DEBIT_SPACE`) and your schema currently **does not exclude redefined fields**, so you can end up with competing fields mapped to the same positions. That can cause “one side wins / other side looks blank” behavior depending on which columns exist in SQL and how you inspect results.

3) **BCP NULL handling**
   - Even when your parser sets Python `None`, in the BCP path you write CSV fields as `""` for missing (`row = [record.get(col, "") ...]`).
   - In SQL, `""` is an empty string, not `NULL`.
   - EFT may “look correct” because those specific fields might not be empty in the data, but MMIS_PROVIDER has many legitimately blank text areas (like `PROV_SERVICE_1`), so it shows up.

But **specifically for your EFT file**: it loads correctly because:
- the XML uses clean group->leaf definitions (no redefines in those parts),
- the leaf names match the SQL columns,
- and the OCCURS naming pattern in SQL matches what your parser generates for those leaves.

If you tell me the exact MMIS_PROVIDER “combined” column name(s) that aren’t loading (the SQL column names you expect, and what values you see), I can pinpoint which of the above is the actual reason—without going into any other pipeline topics.

then i am seeing nulls in teklephpone num and nbank id no

You’re seeing the difference because **your parser only outputs “leaf” XML items (items with no child `<item>` nodes)**, and **your SQL load only inserts the columns that exist in that parsed output**.

So for the **EFT file**, the behavior is:

## Case A (your “split” XML) → works as expected
In this XML, `EFT_BANK_ID_NO` and `EFT_TELEPHONE` are **groups with children**, so the leaf fields are:

- `EFT_BANK_ROUTING_NO` + `EFT_BANK_CHECK_DIGIT`
- `EFT_AREA_CODE` + `EFT_EXCHANGE` + `EFT_PHONE_NUMBER`

Your `parse_cobol_xml()` **adds only these leaf fields to `schema`**, and then `parse_source_line()` produces values for exactly those leaf names. Since your SQL table also has those same leaf column names, they load correctly.

## Case B (your “combined” XML) → nulls appear (but this is actually correct)
In this XML, `EFT_BANK_ID_NO` and `EFT_TELEPHONE` are **leaf fields themselves**:

- `EFT_BANK_ID_NO` (X(9))
- `EFT_TELEPHONE` (X(10))

So the parser outputs only those **combined** columns. That means the parser is *not* producing `EFT_BANK_ROUTING_NO`, `EFT_BANK_CHECK_DIGIT`, `EFT_AREA_CODE`, etc., because they don’t exist in that XML.

### Therefore, you’ll get NULLs depending on what your SQL table expects:

- If your SQL table expects the **split columns** (routing/check-digit/area/exchange/phone), but your XML is the **combined** version, then those split columns will be **NULL** (because parser never produces them).
- If your SQL table expects the **combined columns** (`EFT_BANK_ID_NO`, `EFT_TELEPHONE`) and your XML is the combined version, then those columns will be populated (and they will be NULL only when the source record truly has blanks).

## Why you “see nulls in telephone num and bank id no” with the combined XML/table
If your table really is the combined one (has `EFT_BANK_ID_NO` and `EFT_TELEPHONE`) and you still see NULLs there, then one of these is happening:

1) **The source bytes for those fields are actually blanks** (spaces or NUL padding), and since you enabled `replace_empty_string_with_null`, your cleaning logic turns that into `None` → BCP loads it as NULL.

2) **Your file is being read in byte mode and contains `\x00` padding**, and without cleaning `\x00` to spaces, `.strip()` doesn’t remove it.  
But you already added `_clean_text_value()` with `.replace("\x00", " ").strip()` in your latest parser — that is the right fix for this scenario. With that change, fields that are “all NULs” become empty → then become NULL.

So: **the difference you observe is not a “loading problem” specific to EFT**—it’s simply that the **XML definition determines whether you get split fields or combined fields**, and your SQL table must match that choice 1:1.

If you tell me which of the two EFT table definitions you are *actually* loading into when you observe the NULLs (split-table vs combined-table), I can point to the exact mismatch (XML vs table) that causes it.

from src.pipeline.csv_extract_pipeline import CSVExtractPipeline
from src.pipeline.etl_pipeline import ETLPipeline
from src.pipeline.sor_pipeline import SORPipeline
from src.utils.config_utils import ConfigUtils
from src.utils.execute_ddl import execute_ddl_statements
from src.utils.execute_python_script import execute_shell_command
from src.utils.logging_utils import set_log_prefix, setup_logger

"""
Initial Python script that starts the ETL process and captures the logs. 
Execute this script from the task scheduler to initiate the ETL process.
"""

import argparse
import os

# Set up argument parser
parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Run script with a working directory.")
parser.add_argument("working_dir", help="The working directory for the script")
parser.add_argument("module_name", help="The batch module name to execute")
parser.add_argument("logs_folder", help="The logs folder location")

# Parse arguments
args = parser.parse_args()

# Get the working directory
working_dir = args.working_dir
module_name = args.module_name
logs_folder = args.logs_folder

# Verify it exists
if not os.path.isdir(working_dir):
    print(f"Error: '{working_dir}' is not a valid directory.")
    exit(1)

# Optionally change the script's working directory
os.chdir(working_dir)
print(f"Working directory set to: {os.getcwd()}")


def main():
    set_log_prefix(module_name, logs_folder)

    logger = setup_logger("pipeline")

    config = ConfigUtils()

    etl_pipeline = ETLPipeline()
    sor_pipeline = SORPipeline()
    csv_extract_pipeline = CSVExtractPipeline()
    current_user = os.getlogin()  # Works on most systems
    print(f"Running the job as  : {current_user}")
    # module_name = "ELIGIBILITY_INGEST_JOB"

    job_properties = config.get_job_by_name(module_name)

    if job_properties["job_type"] == "INGEST":
        # Run the ETL pipeline and pass the respective module name
        etl_pipeline.run(module_name)
    elif job_properties["job_type"] == "SOR":
        # Run the SOR pipeline and pass the respective module name
        sor_pipeline.run(module_name)
    elif job_properties["job_type"] == "EXTRACT":
        if job_properties["source"]["type"] == "database":
            # Run the EXTRACT pipeline and pass the respective module name
            csv_extract_pipeline.run(module_name)
    elif job_properties["job_type"] == "REPORTS":
        # Run the ETL pipeline and pass the respective module name
        etl_pipeline.run(module_name)
    elif job_properties["job_type"] == "ADHOC_RUN_SQL":
        execute_ddl_statements(module_name)
    elif job_properties["job_type"] == "ADHOC_RUN_PYTHON_SCRIPT":
        execute_shell_command(module_name)
    elif job_properties["job_type"] == "DB_ALERTS" or job_properties["job_type"] == "MONITOR_ALERTS":
        # Run the ETL pipeline and pass the respective module name
        etl_pipeline.run(module_name)
    else:
        raise Exception(f"Invalid job_type found. {job_properties['job_type']}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

""" def __init__(self): self.extractor = SQLServerExtractor() self.transformer = DataTransformer() self.logger = setup_logger("pipeline") self.csv_generator_utils = CSVGeneratorUtils() self.oracleServerUtils = OracleServerUtils() self.sqlServerUtils = SQLServerUtils() self.config = ConfigUtils() self.settings = get_settings() def run(self, module_name): try: count_source_records = 0 count_target_records = 0 self.logger.info(f"Starting ETL pipeline - {module_name}") log_windows_event( module_name=module_name, event_type=INFO, message=f'Starting ETL pipeline - {module_name}. {datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M")}', ) source_db_type = self.config.get(module_name, "source.connection.type") source_type = self.config.get(module_name, "source.type") # print(f"source_db_type {source_db_type}") # print(f"source_db_type {source_type}") if "ORACLE" == source_db_type: self.oracleServerUtils.migrate_data_from_oracle_to_sql_server( module_name ) elif "ORACLE_THIN_MODE" == source_db_type: # Uses The default "Thin" mode of python-oracledb is comparable to the "Managed" aspect of Oracle.ManagedDataAccess python_oracledb_utils.migrate_data_from_oracle_to_sql_server_using_python_oracledb( module_name ) elif "CUSTOM_SCRIPT" == source_type: self.execute_custom_script(module_name) elif "SQLSERVER" == source_db_type: self.sqlServerUtils.migrate_data_from_sql_server_to_sql_server( module_name ) elif "SFTP" == source_type: sftp_utils.migrate_data_from_sftp_to_sql_server(module_name) elif "DB_ERROR_ALERTS" == source_type: db_health_check_utils.check_db_connections(module_name) elif "MONITOR_SFTP_ERROR_ALERTS" == source_type: health_check_utils_sftp.check_sftp_connections(module_name) elif "MONITOR_SHAREPOINT_ERROR_ALERTS" == source_type: health_check_utils_sharepoint.check_sharepoint_connections(module_name) elif "MONITOR_NETWORK_SHARE_ERROR_ALERTS" == source_type: health_check_utils_network_share.check_network_share_connections(module_name) elif "SFTPCSV" == source_type: sftp_csv_excel_utils.migrate_data_csv_from_sftp_to_sql_server( module_name ) elif "XML_FILE_TO_SSMS" == source_type: split_xml_to_ingestion_ready_utils.run_main_etl_pipeline(module_name) elif "FTS" == source_type: fileShare_utils.migrate_data_from_fts_to_sql_server(module_name) elif "SFTP_EXCEL" == source_type: ftp_utils.migrate_data_from_dss_ftp_to_sql_server(module_name) elif "SHAREPOINT" == source_type: sharepoint_utils.migrate_data_from_sharepoint_to_sql_server(module_name) elif "REPORT" == source_type: excel_csv_reports_utils.migrate_data_from_queries_report_from_config( module_name ) elif "FILESHARE_CSV" == source_type: migrate_data_csv_from_share_to_sql_server( module_name ) elif "CLAIMS" == source_type: migrate_claims_from_sftp_to_sql_server(module_name ) elif "REPORT_TABULAR" == source_type: migrate_data_from_sql_server_to_generate_and_send_email_report( module_name ) elif "ACTIVE_DIRECTORY" == source_type: migrate_data_from_ad_server_to_sql_server(module_name) elif "SFTP_READ" == source_type: sftp_utils.check_for_file_in_sftp_supporting_skipping_failure_email_and_download_to_local(module_name) else: raise Exception(f"Invalid source.type found. {source_type}") self.logger.info(f"ETL pipeline completed successfully - {module_name}") log_windows_event( module_name=module_name, event_type=INFO, message=f"ETL pipeline completed successfully - {module_name}. {datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M')}\n", ) # To catch a custom exception that can be used to suppress sending failure email but will raise exception to exit the process. except CustomSuppressEmailFailureException as e: self.logger.error(f"{module_name}- Custom Exception raised to suppress sending failure email notification: {str(e)}") raise e except Exception as e: # traceback.print_exc() self.logger.error(f"Pipeline failed: {str(e)}") log_windows_event( module_name=module_name, event_type=ERROR, message=f"Pipeline failed {module_name}.\n Exception Details: {str(e)}", alert_type=ALERT_DOH_ADMINS, ) message = "Internal Error Occurred" if isinstance(e, SSHException): self.logger.error(f"Encountered SSH exception and reason for failure is due to {str(e)}") message = f"Internal Error Occurred due to {str(e)[:200]}" asyncio.run( send_failure_notification_just_once( module_name, "ERROR", message ) ) raise e def execute_custom_script(self, module_name: str): started_at = time.time() status = "SUCCESS" message = [] send_email = True try: # Get script path from config and resolve full path script_rel_path = self.config.get(module_name, "source.script_path") script_abs_path = os.path.join(self.settings.BASE_DIR, script_rel_path) self.logger.info(f"Executing custom script for module: {module_name}") self.logger.info(f"Resolved script path: {script_abs_path}") # Extract module name from file path module_name_from_path = os.path.splitext(os.path.basename(script_abs_path))[0] # Dynamically import the script file as a Python module spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location(module_name_from_path, script_abs_path) module = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec) sys.modules[module_name_from_path] = module spec.loader.exec_module(module) # make sure all the custom scripts have got the run method to execute. # Call the 'run()' method with module_name when it exists in script if hasattr(module, "run"): run_fn = getattr(module, "run") self.logger.info(f" Found the run method in custom script. Calling directly for {module_name}.") # building custom message to be added to email notification of AVS Response XML job include_response_in_email = self.config.get(module_name, "source.include_response_in_email") #when we want to append a custom message in email notification for any job include_avs_responses_count_in_email = self.config.get(module_name, "source.include_avs_responses_count_in_email") #specific config to determine the responses count to be added only for response xml job module_type = self.config.get(module_name, "module_type") #custom message below is only for AVS job, so module type is selected below if include_response_in_email and module_type == "AVS" and include_avs_responses_count_in_email: responses_count = run_fn(module_name) custom_message = f'Total number of AVS response files processed till date in the current month: {responses_count}' message.append(custom_message) else: run_fn(module_name) else: raise AttributeError(f"`run()` function not found in script {script_abs_path}") except FileNotFoundError: send_email = False self.logger.error(f"File Not Found error occurred and skipping failure email generation for this exception.") raise CustomSuppressEmailFailureException(f"File Not Found error occurred and skipping failure email generation with custom exception.") except Exception as e: send_email = False self.logger.error(f"Error while executing script for module {module_name}: {e}") raise finally: if send_email: send_email_notification( module_name, status, message, started_at )
